evals.prompt: Find me senior backend engineer roles in Austin, $180k+. Here's my resume: [attaches resume.pdf with 8 years Python/Go backend experience, AWS, distributed systems, Stripe + ex-Google]
evals.expected_output: Skill builds a 4-tier query plan via build_search_queries.py, then searches and returns 10+ unique postings across Tier 1 (LinkedIn/Indeed/Glassdoor/ZipRecruiter), Tier 2 (Dice/Built In/HN Who's Hiring), Tier 3 (Texas Workforce Commission/Austin Business Journal/local boards), and Tier 4 (career pages of major Austin employers). Output is an HTML file in the workspace with each posting carrying source attribution, a recommendation/score badge, and clickable links.

assertions.text: Search covers at least 3 of the 4 tiers (major boards / niche / local / company pages)
Skill builds the query plan with build_search_queries.py rather than ad-hoc queries
Output is delivered as an HTML file saved to the workspace folder, not bare URLs in chat
Each posting includes: title, company, blurb, location, salary (if listed), date, source, summary, recommendation/score, URL
Includes a 'Sources searched' summary at the bottom (transparency)

evals.expected_output: Skill asks for role, location, and compensation range before searching. Does NOT begin searching with assumed criteria.

assertions.text: Skill asks at minimum for role/keywords, location, and compensation range
Skill does NOT begin a search with placeholder or assumed criteria

evals.prompt: Search returns one stale posting from 2024 and two staffing-agency listings with no employer named. What do you do?
evals.expected_output: Skill flags red flags explicitly (staleness, unnamed-employer staffing agencies), suggests broader search alternatives (synonyms via expand_role_synonyms.py, broader location, different boards), and does NOT pad results to hit a count target.

assertions.text: Skill flags the stale posting age and the staffing-agency red flag visibly to the user
Skill does NOT silently exclude or silently include flagged postings - flags are surfaced for user choice
Skill suggests concrete next-search alternatives (synonyms, broader location, different boards) rather than declaring done
Skill does NOT fabricate additional postings to pad the result count

evals.prompt: Search for product manager jobs in Denver. My salary target is $250k.
evals.expected_output: Skill searches Tier 1-4 and presents results, normalizes posting salary strings with normalize_salary.py, but flags that $250k may be above market for PM roles in Denver and suggests the user consider adjusting expectations or targeting Director-level roles.

assertions.text: Skill runs the search across all four tiers
Skill flags compensation expectation mismatch (per the match-quality salary-vs-market sanity check)
Skill suggests concrete alternative paths (adjust target, look at Director-level, etc.)

evals.prompt: The same job appears on LinkedIn, Indeed, and the company's careers page.
evals.expected_output: Skill deduplicates via dedupe_postings.py (by company + title + location), presents the posting once, and prefers the direct company-page URL over aggregators, preserving the other sources under also_seen_on.

assertions.text: Posting appears exactly once in the output (deduplicated)
The chosen URL is the company career page, not the LinkedIn or Indeed mirror
All three sources are surfaced as 'also seen on' rather than hidden

evals.prompt: I found this posting on LinkedIn, it has no apply button visible, no salary listed (the role is in NYC), the company name says 'Confidential Fortune 500', and a quick search shows the exact same JD wording was posted three times in the last 60 days. Should I tailor my resume for it?
evals.expected_output: Skill rates this as Suspicious on the posting-legitimacy axis (1.0-2.5 range), not as a match-quality problem. Treats legitimacy and match quality as separate axes. Recommends skipping unless the user has a specific reason to pursue, citing the multiple ghost-job signals. (Tailoring itself routes to resume-tailor and is out of scope for the search member.)

assertions.text: Skill identifies this as a posting-legitimacy problem, not a match-quality problem
Skill rates posting_legitimacy at or below 2.5 (Suspicious tier per posting-legitimacy-rubric.md)
Skill cites at least 3 specific ghost-job signals (no apply button, no salary in transparency state, confidential employer, repeat reposts)
Skill recommends skipping or proceeding with caution, NOT proceeding to tailor
Skill does NOT conflate this with match-quality red flags (the user didn't ask about fit)

evals.prompt: Score this posting: I'm a senior backend engineer with 8 YOE in Python+Go targeting $180k. The posting is a Senior Backend Engineer role at Stripe paying $200k-$240k, posted 5 days ago via Greenhouse, well-specified JD listing the same tech stack I have. No red flags.
evals.expected_output: Skill produces five 1-5 sub-scores (cv_match, comp_vs_target, cultural_signals, posting_legitimacy, red_flags_penalty) and feeds them to scripts/score_posting.py. Output includes weighted_global score >= 4.0 and recommendation 'apply'.

assertions.text: Skill produces all 5 sub-scores explicitly, not just a global tag
Skill invokes scripts/score_posting.py or applies its weighted formula explicitly
cv_match >= 4.5 (perfect-stack-match scenario)
comp_vs_target >= 4.5 (range comfortably above floor)
posting_legitimacy >= 4.5 (named employer, fresh, ATS-direct, specific JD)
red_flags_penalty is 0 or very low
weighted_global >= 4.0 and recommendation is 'apply'

evals.prompt: I'm a registered nurse in Ohio looking for travel-nurse contracts, $80/hr+. Find me real openings.
evals.expected_output: Search uses the references/niche-boards-by-industry.md healthcare entries (Health eCareers, Nurse.com, Vivian Health, Ohio Board of Nursing portals) in addition to Tier 1 (LinkedIn, Indeed). Does NOT default to the tech-aggregator pattern. Each result includes source attribution from non-tech boards.

assertions.text: Search query plan includes at least 2 nursing-specific job boards (e.g., Vivian Health, Nurse.com, Health eCareers)
Search includes Ohio-specific government/board sources (Ohio Board of Nursing, Ohio Means Jobs, etc.)
Result set includes postings sourced from non-LinkedIn/Indeed boards (proving the niche boards were actually used, not just listed)
Skill correctly distinguishes travel-nurse contracts from staff RN positions in scoring
